GHSA-jrp5-g662-hq92
Unsafe ZIP Entry Names (Zip Slip Class) in Archive Download Endpoint
Low
bo0tzz
published
GHSA-jrp5-g662-hq92
Jul 6, 2026
Package
npm
immich
(
npm
)
Affected versions
>2.6.2
Patched versions
3.0.0
Description
Immich – Unsafe ZIP Entry Names (Zip Slip Class) in Archive Download Endpoint
Title
Unsafe ZIP Entry Names (Zip Slip Class) in Archive Download Endpoint
Product
Immich
Version
Immich Server v2.6.2
Vulnerability Type
Zip Slip / Path Traversal via Archive Entry Names
CWE
CWE-22 – Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory
CWE-73 – External Control of File Name or Path
CVSS v3.1 (Suggested)
Score:
5.4 (Medium)
Vector:
AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:U/C:N/I:L/A:N
Date
2026-03-26
Credit
RegorSec (Roger Bourget)
Summary
Immich generates ZIP archives for asset downloads using user-controlled filenames as ZIP entry names without sufficient sanitization or normalization. A user who uploads an asset with a crafted filename can cause generated ZIP archives to contain entries with path components such as directory traversal sequences or absolute paths.
This represents a Zip Slip–class vulnerability where downstream archive extraction tools may write files outside the intended extraction directory depending on extractor behavior and platform.
At minimum, this violates the expectation that generated ZIP archives contain safe relative filenames.
Affected Component
Archive generation endpoint:
POST /api/download/archive
Relevant code path:
DownloadService.downloadArchive()
→ StorageRepository.createZipStream()
→ archiver.file(input, { name: filename })
The archive entry name is derived from:
asset.originalFileName
which originates from a user-controlled upload filename.
Root Cause
The upload process allows a user-controlled filename to be stored as
asset.originalFileName
without path sanitization. This value is later used directly as the ZIP entry name when generating archives.
Observed behavior indicates:
No basename enforcement
No filtering of traversal sequences (
../
)
No filtering of Windows path separators
No normalization of absolute paths or drive paths
As a result, attacker-controlled filenames are embedded directly into ZIP archive entry paths.
Proof of Concept / Observations
Using authenticated upload and archive download functionality, the following behaviors were observed:
Uploaded Filename
ZIP Entry Name
../../../../tmp/pwn.jpg
tmp/pwn.jpg
C:\temp\abs3.jpg
temp/abs3.jpg
a/../../b.jpg
a/../../b.jpg
....\win1.jpg
win1.jpg
Some traversal sequences are normalized into subdirectories, while others remain intact in the ZIP entry name.
This demonstrates inconsistent path handling and confirms attacker control over ZIP entry paths.
Impact
Potential impacts include:
File overwrite during extraction depending on unzip tool behavior
Writing files outside intended extraction directory
Malicious archive contents placing files into unexpected directories
Risk when archives are shared via links, email, or downloads
Client-side path traversal upon extraction
Severity depends on the extraction tool and environment used by the victim.
This is primarily a client-side archive extraction vulnerability.
Reproduction Steps
Authenticate to Immich and obtain access token.
Upload an asset using a crafted filename:
POST /api/assets
multipart form field:
filename = ../../../../tmp/pwn.jpg
Request archive download:
POST /api/download/archive
{
  "assetIds": ["<assetId>"]
}
Extract the generated ZIP archive.
Observe archive entry paths:
zipinfo -1 archive.zip
Recommended Remediation
Sanitize filenames used for ZIP entry names.
Recommended approaches:
Use
basename()
only
Strip
/
and
\
Reject
..
Reject absolute paths
Reject Windows drive paths
Enforce allowlist filename regex
Optionally generate server-side archive filenames instead of using user input
Notes
This behavior was observed in a lab environment running an Immich server container. Exact behavior may vary depending on Immich version and ZIP library path normalization behavior.
Severity
Low
2.8
CVSS overall score
This score calculates overall vulnerability severity from 0 to 10 and is based on the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS).
/ 10
CVSS v3 base metrics
Attack vector
Local
Attack complexity
Low
Privileges required
Low
User interaction
Required
Scope
Unchanged
Confidentiality
None
Integrity
Low
Availability
None
Learn more about base metrics
CVSS v3 base metrics
Attack vector:
More severe the more the remote (logically and physically) an attacker can be in order to exploit the vulnerability.
Attack complexity:
More severe for the least complex attacks.
Privileges required:
More severe if no privileges are required.
User interaction:
More severe when no user interaction is required.
Scope:
More severe when a scope change occurs, e.g. one vulnerable component impacts resources in components beyond its security scope.
Confidentiality:
More severe when loss of data confidentiality is highest, measuring the level of data access available to an unauthorized user.
Integrity:
More severe when loss of data integrity is the highest, measuring the consequence of data modification possible by an unauthorized user.
Availability:
More severe when the loss of impacted component availability is highest.
CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:U/C:N/I:L/A:N
CVE ID
No known CVE
Weaknesses
Weakness
CWE-22
Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
The product uses external input to construct a pathname that is intended to identify a file or directory that is located underneath a restricted parent directory, but the product does not properly neutralize special elements within the pathname that can cause the pathname to resolve to a location that is outside of the restricted directory.
Learn more on MITRE.
Weakness
CWE-73
External Control of File Name or Path
The product allows user input to control or influence paths or file names that are used in filesystem operations.
Learn more on MITRE.
Credits
regorsec
Reporter
